revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:羨道]][[ko:널길]]
[[File:Hakuchoduka Kohun.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Shiratorizuka Kofun (Takarazuka)|ja|白鳥塚古墳 (宝塚市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiratorizuka Kofun (Takarazuka)|qq=}} ({{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, {{ill|Takarazuka, Hyōgo|en|Takarazuka, Hyōgo|qid=Q823967|lt=Takarazuka|1=Takarazuka, Hyōgo}})羨道]] [[File:HachimanYama-Kofun Saitama.jpg|thumb|The passage, antechamber, middle chamber, and main chamber of {{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Gyoda)|ja|八幡山古墳 (行田市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachimanyama Kofun (Gyoda)|qq=}} ({{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|qid=Q128186|lt=Saitama|1=Saitama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Gyōda|en|Gyōda|qid=Q860659|lt=Gyōda|1=Gyōda}}). The stone chamber is exposed due to loss of mound soil.]] [[File:Tenmondai-Kounai Kofun excavation.jpg|thumb|Dual-chamber structure of {{ill|Tenmondai Kofun|ja|天文台構内古墳|1=Tenmondai Kounai Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondai Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondai Kounai Kofun|qq=}} ({{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, {{ill|Mitaka, Tokyo|en|Mitaka, Tokyo|qid=Q242885|lt=Mitaka|1=Mitaka, Tokyo}}), with a wide burial area in the foreground. The passage is extremely short, measuring only 10–20 centimeters.]] [[File:Kinreizuka-2.jpg|thumb|150px|Stone chamber without a clear distinction between passage and chamber at {{ill|Kinreizuka Kofun|ja|金鈴塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinreizuka Kofun|qq=}} ({{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|qid=Q80011|lt=Chiba|1=Chiba Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kisarazu|en|Kisarazu|qid=Q335427|lt=Kisarazu|1=Kisarazu}})]] [[File:Ishibutai-kofun Asuka Nara pref05n4592.jpg|thumb|The famous {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|en|Ishibutai Kofun|qid=Q3816159|lt=Ishibutai Kofun|1=Ishibutai Kofun}} with its massive capstone ({{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara|1=Nara Prefecture}}, {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|qid=Q752397|lt=Asuka|1=Asuka, Nara}})]] '''Endō''' (羨道) refers to the passage connecting the burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu) of a [[kofun]] with the external environment. Commonly found in [[horizontal stone chambers]] and [[Yokoanabo|yokohama tombs]], it is also known as "sendō" in some contexts.<ref>Ōtsuka & Tozawa (1996)</ref><ref name=tanboh>Tanmura & Gōda (2000)</ref> == Overview == Endōs are passageways forming part of the burial structure in horizontal stone chambers (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu), often enclosed within mounds or tomb entrances. The entrance is referred to as the "end gate" (羨門, senmon). Unlike Chinese Kofuns, which often feature exposed pathways, Japanese endōs are typically roofed.<ref name="#1">Ōtsuka & Kobayashi (1982), p.365</ref> The passage is narrower and lower in height than the main burial chamber. Horizontal stone chambers with multiple rooms, termed "dual-chamber" (複室構造, fukushitsu kōzō), distinguish the chambers as "antechamber" (前室, zenshitsu) and "main chamber" (奥室, okusitsu). Some tombs also feature intermediate chambers or lack clear demarcations.<ref name="#1"/> The endō sometimes served ceremonial functions, and evidence suggests that burial rituals took place within the passage. End gates were typically sealed with stones, wooden doors, or clay barriers, which could be removed for subsequent burials.<ref name=tanboh/> == Origins and Functions == === Emergence and Diffusion === The endō first appeared as part of horizontal stone burial chambers in [[Kyushu]] during the late {{ill|4th century|en|4th century|qid=Q8099|lt=4th century|1=4th century}}.<ref name=habu111>Habuta (1992), p.111</ref> These structures are believed to have been influenced by {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|qid=Q18097|lt=Korean|1=Korea}} burial practices. Initially concentrated along the Genkai Sea coastline, their distribution expanded to central and eastern Japan by the 5th century.<ref name=habu111/> The {{ill|6th century|en|6th century|qid=Q8090|lt=6th century|1=6th century}} saw the introduction of the "Kinai-style chamber" (畿内型石室, Kinai-gata sekishitsu) in the Kinki region, triggering widespread adoption of horizontal stone chambers across Japan.<ref name=habu111/> === Construction Methods === The construction of an endō follows these steps:<ref name=wada>Wada (1992), pp.54-55</ref> 1. Excavation of the mound and placement of foundational stones for the chamber walls. 2. Installation of burial artifacts such as wooden or Sarcophaguss. 3. Sequential layering of stones in the chamber and passageway. 4. Placement of ceiling stones over the chamber and passage. 5. Closure of the entrance with sealing stones or doors. Many entrances face south, but some early examples within keyhole-shaped tombs align with the mound's front section or constricted areas, showing no directional restrictions.<ref name=jiten>Ōtsuka & Kobayashi (1982), p.365</ref> === Symbolic Roles === Endōs provided accessibility for secondary burials, contrasting with {{ill|Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|lt=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|lt=Vertical stone chamber|10=ja|11=竪穴式石室|lt=vertical stone chambers|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/vertical stone chamber|qq=}} (竪穴式石室, tateana-shiki sekishitsu). Many chambers served as family tombs, containing remains of multiple individuals.<ref name=habu112>Habuta (1992), p.112</ref> The adoption of horizontal chambers also marked a shift in spiritual beliefs. While earlier tombs emphasized ascension to the heavens, horizontal structures symbolized the underworld. Burial artifacts such as food and pottery reflect offerings for the afterlife.<ref name=habu2003>Habuta (2003), pp.19-21</ref> == Gallery == <gallery> File:Yataotsuka 02.JPG|{{ill|Yata Ōtsuka Kofun|en|Yata Ōtsuka Kofun|qid=Q11603302|lt=Yata Ōtsuka Kofun|1=Yata Ōtsuka Kofun}} ({{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|qid=Q132936|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kurashiki|en|Kurashiki|qid=Q467487|lt=Kurashiki|1=Kurashiki}}) File:oumakiichigoukohun-2.JPG|View from the endō of {{ill|Ōmaki No.1 Kofun|ja|大牧1号古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmaki No.1 Kofun|qq=}} ({{ill|Gifu Prefecture|en|Gifu Prefecture|qid=Q131277|lt=Gifu|1=Gifu Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kakamigahara|en|Kakamigahara|qid=Q641491|lt=Kakamigahara|1=Kakamigahara}}) File:Okameishi.jpg|Stone endō of {{ill|Okameishi Kofun|en|Okameishi Kofun|qid=Q11262874|lt=Okameishi Kofun|1=Okameishi Kofun}} ({{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Tondabayashi|en|Tondabayashi|qid=Q490928|lt=Tondabayashi|1=Tondabayashi}}) File:Nishitsuzuka-kofun01.jpg|Entrance to the endō of {{ill|Nishizuka Kofun|en|Nishizuka Kofun|qid=Q18337677|lt=Nishizuka Kofun|1=Nishizuka Kofun}} ({{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|en|Yao, Osaka|qid=Q490872|lt=Yao|1=Yao, Osaka}}) File:Nishitsuzuka-kofun02.jpg|Trapezoidal endō of Nishitsuzuka Kofun </gallery> == References == {{Cite book|language=ja|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige, Kobayashi Saburō|title=Dictionary of Kofun|publisher=Tōkyōdō Publishing|year=1982}} {{Cite book|language=ja|author=Wada Susumu|title=The Age of Kofun|publisher=Shōgakukan|year=1992}} {{Cite book|language=ja|author=Habuta Junshi|title=Introduction to Kofun Studies Nationwide|publisher=Gakuseisha|year=2003}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} * {{ill|Passage grave|en|Passage grave|qid=Q1426772|lt=Passage grave|1=Passage grave}} == References ==
